source_metadata.title_raw: Michael J. Maurer - A-19-007 - April 5, 2019 - Los Angeles

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 5, 2019

Michael J. Maurer
Partner
Best Best & Krieger LLP
300 South Grand Avenue, 25th Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-007

Dear Mr. Maurer:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Lafayette's Parks, Trails and Recreation Commission ("PTR"), PTR Director Jonathan Katayanagi, and PTR Commissioners Lauren McCabe Herpich and Mark Poole, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Does Commissioner Herpich have a conflict of interest in discussing the "Park Theater" if she has advocated for restoration of that site in the past as a private citizen and discussed the private funding of such restoration?

2. May Commissioner Poole participate in a discussion of various potential park sites, as more specifically explained below, if one of the potential sites is within 500 feet of his real property?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Because the Act only concerns financial conflicts of interest, and Commissioner Herpich has no financial interest in this matter, she does not have a conflict of interest under the Act.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-007
Page No. 2

2. Based on the facts provided, it is reasonably foreseeable that the discussion and ranking of potential park sites will involve the Hamlin Road site. As Commissioner Poole's real property is located within 500 feet of this site, his financial interest prohibits his participation in PTR decisions because it is reasonably foreseeable that they will have a material financial effect on his residential property.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Commissioner Herpich

The PTR may consider recommending that the City either acquire a site known as the "Park Theater" or otherwise take official action to restore and re-open the Park Theater. Commissioner Herpich does not live within 500 feet of Park Theater. In 2016, Commissioner Herpich met with several other interested residents to discuss creating a group to put together a plan to help reopen Park Theater. Commissioner Herpich volunteered to manage the "Save Lafayette Park Theater" Facebook page and has been managing it since. A citizens group, which included Commissioner Herpich, discussed opportunities for funding the purchase of the theater, potentially as a public-private partnership, using some measure of private investment. Commissioner Herpich did not make any investment in the group or pay any funds to be used for an acquisition of Park Theater.

Commissioner Poole

Commissioner Poole owns property that is approximately 450 feet away from a site known as the "Hamlin Road" site. The site is not currently a City park, but the PTR may discuss whether to recommend to the City Council to acquire the site for parkland uses.

There will be a two-step process to the recommendation. The first step will be more of a "study session." In order to provide for transparency, public participation, and community involvement, the PTR would hold a discussion for commissioners, staff, and members of the public to propose/suggest possible opportunities within the City. The suggested opportunities would be posted on the wall in the meeting room (or another public location in the meeting room) so that the PTR could discuss prioritizing the potential locations. The commissioners may be asked to "rank" their preferred locations or otherwise identify preferred locations. However, the PTR will not be making recommendation at this meeting. The second step will be a future meeting where the PTR makes an actual recommendation to the City Council. You note that the request for advice only applies to the initial meeting (the first step), where there will be no actual recommendation made.

The initial agenda item will not identify the Hamlin Road site, but the discussion might (or might not) eventually include the Hamlin Road site.

Background of PTR

The City Council created the PTR by ordinance, which is codified in Lafayette Municipal Code Chapter 2-8. The PTR is an advisory commission, consisting of nine members appointed by the City Council. At least eight of the nine members must be residents of the City, and all members must have demonstrated knowledge of and interest in parks, trails, and/or recreation. LMC section 2-809 sets forth the following PTR duties:

File No. A-19-007
Page No. 3

The commission shall:

(a) Propose for the consideration of the city council a municipal parks and recreation
program, including the acquisition, development and maintenance of park and recreation
facilities, and the operation of a recreational program, together with one or more sources of
continuing revenue (in addition to current city revenues, except for parkland dedication fees)
sufficient to fund the proposal;

(b) On a continuing basis, collect and disseminate information on the park and recreation
facilities and programs available to Lafayette citizens;

(c) Periodically analyze the needs of the community for recreational facilities of all types,
both public and private, including profit-making recreational businesses, and recommend to
the city council appropriate municipal involvement in the financing, acquisition,
construction and operation of such facilities;

(d) Advise the city council on the operation of such recreational facilities as may be
acquired or leased by the city, including recommendations as to user fees and facility rental
charges;

(e) Prepare, and from time to time, as needed, revise a written policy governing the use of
city-owned recreational facilities and parklands, and, upon the approval of such policy
statement by the city council, supervise and control the use of such facilities in compliance
with the approved policy;

(f) Recommend appropriate action by the city council on proposed gifts of real property,
money and other valuable assets, when the gift is to be used for recreational purposes;

(g) Solicit on behalf of the city gifts and grants for recreational purposes and recommend
appropriate action by the city council on applications for such grants;

(h) Encourage private individuals and organizations to (1) provide recreational facilities for
the benefit of the community; and (2) subsidize the operation of those facilities;

(i) Perform such advisory tasks related to parks and recreation as may from time to time be
assigned by the city council;

(j) Provide liaison in park and recreation matters between the city and other public and
quasi-public agencies which own facilities which can be used for recreation, and/or which
conduct recreation activities;

(k) Advise and guide civic groups, youth organizations and interested individuals who wish
to engage in community service projects in the field of parks and recreation; and

(l) Advise city staff in such park and recreation activities as the city council decides to
undertake.

File No. A-19-007
Page No. 4

The City of Lafayette's Conflict of Interest Code designates PTR commissioners as Form 700 filers,
who are required to disclose:

Category 1: All investments and business positions in business entities and sources of
income, including gifts, loans and travel payments, that are located in, that do business in or
that own real property within the jurisdiction of the City; and

Category 2: All interests in real property which is located in whole or in part within, or not
more than two (2) miles outside, the jurisdiction of the City.²

ANALYSIS

Commissioner Herpich

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.

Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial
effect on one or more of the official's interests, which include:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

² We note that you have also sought guidance regarding disqualification based upon undetermined park
locations such as a location subsequently identified by the public. Generally, disqualification is required if it is
reasonably foreseeable the decision will have a material financial effect. The determination of whether an effect on a
currently undetermined location is reasonably foreseeable cannot be made without an understanding of all pertinent
information known to the official. To the extent there is no reason to believe a location will be identified, an official is
not disqualified. However, once a location is identified, an official with property within 1,000 feet of the decision is
potentially disqualified and an official with property 500 feet or less from the decision is generally disqualified. (See
Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) and (8).) If an official needs additional assistance based upon a specifically identified
property, the official should seek further advice at that time. However, we note that it may be possible to segment a
decision, if an official is disqualified, as discussed in Footnote 4.

File No. A-19-007
Page No. 5

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family, also known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

Conflicts of interests under the Act are based on the above-referenced financial effects.
Because the Act only regulates conflicts of interest with respect to financial interests, and you have
not identified any financial interest in the governmental decision, Commissioner Herpich would not
have a conflict of interest under the Act.

Commissioner Poole

Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are set forth in Section 87103.
The interest pertinent to the officials in question is set forth in Section 87103(b): "An interest in real
property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more." (Section
87103(b).)

In order for an interest in real property to be disqualifying, the specific decision in question
must have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the property. Regulation 18701(a) provides
that a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or
the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial
interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as
described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in a
decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Commissioner Poole's interests are not explicitly involved in the
decisions at issue.

Additionally, Regulation 18702.2(a)(7)³ provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial
interest is material whenever the governmental decision involves property located 500 feet or less
from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision
will not have any measurable impact on the official's parcel.

You have asked about Commissioner Poole's involvement in decisions regarding the
location of a park and ranking locations for a park and have identified the Hamelin Road Site as a
potential location. While the site may or may not be actually discussed as an alternative, it appears
reasonably foreseeable based on the facts presented that the discussion will involve the Hamlin
Road Site.⁴ As Commissioner Poole's real property is located within 500 feet of this site, his

³ The Commission adopted amendments to Regulation 18702.2 at its January 17, 2019 meeting, and this
analysis applies that regulation as amended.

⁴Under certain circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other related
decisions provided that the official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of
interest. For "segmentation" to be permitted, "the decision in which the official has a financial interest [must be capable
of being] broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official
has a disqualifying financial interest." (18706(a)(1).) Accordingly, if the PTR first votes to approve the Hamelin Road
Site as a potential site without Commissioner Poole's taking part in the decision, Commissioner Poole is not

File No. A-19-007
Page No. 6

financial interest prohibits his participation in PTR decisions because it is reasonably foreseeable
that they will have a material financial effect on his residential property, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally. (Regulations 18702.2 and 18703.) Moreover, you have not presented
any facts indicating that there is clear and convincing evidence that the decisions will not have any
measurable impact on Commissioner Poole's property interest.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

disqualified from taking part in the subsequent discussions of park locations assuming no other potentially disqualifying
conflicts of interests in the discussions.

QUESTION: 1. Does Commissioner Herpich have a conflict of interest in discussing the "Park Theater" if 
she has advocated for restoration of that site in the past as a private citizen and discussed the private 
funding of such restoration? 
2. May Commissioner Poole participate in a discussion of various potential park sites, as 
more specifically explained below, if one of the potential sites is within 500 feet of his real 
property?

CONCLUSION: 1. Because the Act only concerns financial conflicts of interest, and Commissioner Herpich 
has no financial interest in this matter, she does not have a conflict of interest under the Act.2. Based on the facts provided, it is reasonably foreseeable that the discussion and ranking 
of potential park sites will involve the Hamlin Road site. As Commissioner Poole's real property is 
located within 500 feet of this site, his financial interest prohibits his participation in PTR decisions 
because it is reasonably foreseeable that they will have a material financial effect on his residential 
property.
